title: Origami envelope, folding a square into a sealed letter holder
subtitle: A traditional Japanese mailing envelope folded from a single square sheet
excerpt: The origami envelope (fumikiri in Japanese) is a traditional method of folding a letter and its envelope from a single sheet of paper. The finished envelope is self-sealing with a neat diagonal flap. It requires no adhesive and produces a piece that is both functional and decorative.
sourceNotes: Traditional Japanese origami. The fumikiri envelope has been documented in Japanese paper-folding traditions since the Edo period. Instructions for the construction appear in 19th-century Japanese paper-craft manuals.

glossaryTerms.definition: A foundational origami manoeuvre in which all four corners of a square are folded to meet at the centre point. The result is a smaller square with four triangular flaps. Many traditional models begin with a blintz fold.
An envelope closure method in which the final flap is folded over and its tip tucked under an adjacent flap. No adhesive is required, the tension of the folded layers holds the closure in place.

content.text: The origami envelope uses a partial
blintz fold
as its basis and closes with a
tuck closure

. It works with any square sheet; the envelope opening is roughly one third of the square's diagonal, so plan for a square at least twice the size of what it needs to hold. A square cut from A4 paper (210 x 210 mm) produces an envelope that holds a small folded note or a gift card.
Step 1, Crease the diagonals and centre lines

Place the square coloured side down. Fold in half horizontally, crease, and unfold. Fold in half vertically, crease, and unfold. Fold corner to corner along both diagonals, crease, and unfold. You now have eight crease lines radiating from the centre.
Step 2, Partial blintz: fold three corners to centre
This envelope uses a partial
blintz fold
rather than a full one. Fold the bottom corner, left corner, and right corner of the square up to meet the centre point. Crease each fold firmly. Leave the top corner unfolded for now.
Step 3, Fold the side corners inward
Fold the left and right outer edges of the model inward so their edges meet at the centre vertical line. The model is now a tall rectangle with a pointed bottom. Crease the side folds firmly.
Step 4, Form the envelope body
Fold the pointed bottom of the rectangle upward so the point meets the top edge of the model. This creates the front flap of the envelope body and locks the side folds in place. Crease firmly.
Step 5, Close with the tuck
Fold the remaining top corner down over the front of the model. The pointed tip should reach into the pocket formed by the folded bottom flap. Tuck the tip into this pocket to complete the
tuck closure
. No adhesive is required. To open the envelope, simply lift the top flap and withdraw the tucked point.
Paper selection

Standard origami paper at 70-80 gsm produces a clean envelope. Heavier paper (above 120 gsm) resists the blintz folds and produces an uneven pocket. Patterned paper works well since the inside of the envelope is hidden, the outside shows only the final flap and two side edges of the chosen paper.
